questions.q: Which of the following is included in a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP)?
options: Value of domestically produced final goods and services in a given year
Total income earned by citizens regardless of location
Total wealth accumulated by the nation over all time
All financial transactions including stock market trades
questions.explanation: GDP measures the market value of final goods and services produced within a country during a specific period. Option B describes Gross National Income (location of earner, not production). Stock market trades (option D) represent transfers of existing assets, not new production, so they're excluded from GDP.

options: A sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services over time
An increase in the quantity of money in the economy
A decrease in the purchasing power of savings
An increase in the cost of imported goods
questions.explanation: Inflation describes the rate at which average prices rise, reducing each unit of money's purchasing power. While inflation often accompanies money supply increases (option B), the increase in money is a potential cause, not the definition. Purchasing power decline (option C) is a consequence, not the definition.

questions.q: The natural rate of unemployment represents:
options: The rate of unemployment when the economy is at full employment with only frictional and structural unemployment
Zero percent unemployment, meaning no one is jobless
The maximum unemployment rate a country can sustain
The average unemployment rate over the past 10 years
questions.explanation: The natural rate (around 4-5% in developed economies) includes frictional unemployment (job search) and structural unemployment (skill mismatches), but excludes cyclical unemployment from recessions. It represents full employment capacity. Option B is impossible due to normal job transitions; option C confuses maximum with natural rate.

options: Increased government spending and/or decreased taxes to stimulate economic growth
Decreased government spending and increased taxes to reduce inflation
A reduction in the money supply to control price increases
Increasing interest rates to make borrowing more expensive
questions.explanation: Expansionary policy aims to boost aggregate demand during weak economic periods. Increased spending and lower taxes both leave more money in the economy. Option B describes contractionary fiscal policy. Options C and D describe monetary policy tools, not fiscal policy.

questions.q: What does the business cycle typically consist of?
options: Periods of expansion, peak, contraction, and trough
Only expansion and recession phases
A steady state with no fluctuations
Random economic movements unrelated to each other
questions.explanation: The business cycle has four standard phases: expansion (rising output), peak (maximum point), contraction/recession (falling output), and trough (lowest point). Option B omits the peak and trough. Option C ignores the reality of economic fluctuations that characterize modern economies.

questions.q: Stagflation refers to an economic condition with:
options: High inflation and high unemployment occurring simultaneously
Very low inflation with very low unemployment
High inflation with falling unemployment
High unemployment with falling inflation
questions.explanation: Stagflation combines stagn­ation (high unemployment) with inflation, an unusual but difficult condition experienced in the 1970s. Normal Phillips curve relationship suggests inverse unemployment-inflation tradeoff, making simultaneous high rates problematic. Option B describes favorable conditions; option C and D describe normal tradeoffs.

questions.q: According to the quantity theory of money, if the money supply doubles and velocity and output remain constant, what happens to the price level?
options: The price level approximately doubles
The price level remains unchanged
The price level falls by half
The price level increases by less than 50%
questions.explanation: The equation of exchange (MV = PQ) shows money supply × velocity = price level × quantity. If M doubles while V and Q stay constant, P must double to maintain equality. This illustrates the long-run relationship between money growth and inflation, though short-run effects are more complex.

options: Increased government spending raises interest rates, reducing private investment
Government spending completely eliminates private sector activity
Consumers reduce spending due to lower taxes
The central bank reduces the money supply
questions.explanation: Crowding out happens when government borrowing to finance spending drives up interest rates in the loanable funds market, making private borrowing more expensive and reducing private investment. Option B overstates the effect—complete elimination rarely occurs. Options C and D describe different mechanisms than crowding out.

questions.q: The aggregate demand curve slopes downward because:
options: Higher price levels reduce real wealth and purchasing power, decreasing consumption and investment
Consumers always buy less when prices increase
The government reduces spending when inflation rises
Supply constraints force producers to raise prices
questions.explanation: The AD curve slopes down due to three effects: real wealth effect (higher prices reduce real asset value), interest rate effect (higher prices increase money demand, raising rates), and net exports effect (higher domestic prices reduce exports). Option B confuses individual good demand curves with aggregate demand, which involves price-level changes, not individual price effects.

questions.q: Which situation would cause the aggregate supply curve to shift leftward in the short run?
options: An increase in the price of oil used in production
A decrease in the general price level
An increase in government spending
An improvement in technology
questions.explanation: A leftward AS shift indicates decreased supply capacity. Oil price increases raise production costs, reducing output at each price level. Option B describes movement along the curve, not a shift. Option C affects demand, not supply. Option D shifts AS rightward by improving productivity.
